1: A type: "Greatly have they afflicted me from my youth." The afflicted righteous one who suffers throughout life but is not ultimately overcome. Christ was opposed from birth (Matthew 2:13-16, Herod's slaughter) through his entire ministry to the cross. Yet the affliction did not defeat him — Acts 2:24 ("it was not possible for him to be held by death"). John 16:33 — "I have overcome the world."
2: A type: "Greatly have they afflicted me from my youth, yet they have not prevailed against me." The repeated emphasis — afflicted, yet not defeated — is the pattern of Christ's suffering and vindication. The "yet" is the resurrection. All that the powers of darkness did to him did not prevail (Colossians 2:15 — "he made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them by the cross").
3: A type: "The plowers plowed upon my back; they made long their furrows." The scourging of Christ. John 19:1 — Pilate had Jesus flogged. Isaiah 50:6 — "I gave my back to those who strike." Isaiah 53:5 — "by his wounds we are healed." The "long furrows" on the back of the sufferer are the stripes of the passion through which Christ bore our griefs and carried our sorrows.
4: A shadow: "The LORD is righteous; he has cut the cords of the wicked." Christ's death cuts the cords that bound humanity to condemnation and slavery. Colossians 2:14-15 — "having canceled the charge of our legal indebtedness, which stood against us and condemned us; he has taken it away, nailing it to the cross." The cords of the wicked are the debt of sin, cut at Calvary.
5: A revelation of God: "May all who hate Zion be put to shame and turned backward!" God's enemies will be made "a footstool for your feet" — Psalms 110:1 (quoted in Hebrews 1:13). 1 Corinthians 15:25 — "he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet." The enemies of Zion who have been put to shame is the accomplished work of the cross and the completing work of Christ's reign.
6: A revelation of God: the wicked are "like the grass on the housetops, which withers before it grows up." Christ uses similar imagery of the fruitless and the fruitful — John 15:6 ("If anyone does not abide in me he is thrown away like a branch and withers"); Matthew 13:5-6 (the seed without root springs up but withers). Those who oppose the Lord are like unrooted grass — impressive for a moment, gone entirely.
7: A revelation of God: the grass that "does not fill the hand of the reaper or the arms of him who binds sheaves." The harvest imagery contrasts the fruitless wicked with the fruitful righteous — the full harvest that Christ brings in (Matthew 13:30,39-43: the final harvest; John 4:35 — "the fields are white for harvest"). What the wicked cannot produce, Christ produces abundantly.
8: A revelation of God: "The blessing of the LORD not be on them!" — those who pass by do not say "the blessing of the LORD be upon you." The final state of those who reject God is exclusion from his blessing. The inverse is the great blessing Christ pronounces over his own (Matthew 5:3-12 — the Beatitudes). The psalm's negative and positive are both realized in the pronouncements of Christ the judge (Matthew 25:34,41).
